Motion To Tax Csaas Memorandum Of Costs Declaration Of Fredrick A Hagen
Off Calendar Sep-19-2022 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances via Zoom Conference
Fredrick A. Hagen, ESQ., BERDING & WEIL LLP, 925-838-2090 for Plaintiffs, Shacuan Sharon Seto; Gordon Lee; Patricia Lum; Stephen K. Lee; Golden Prosperities Management Company, LLC; and Stephen K. Lee Enterprises,
Min K. Kang. Esq, CODDINGTON, HICKS & DANFORTH, 650-592-5400, Defendants CSAA Firre & Casualty Insurance Company and CSAA Insurance Exchange for Defendant.
Nancy Presant, CSR 906, 510-751-8550, npresantcsr@gmail.com Court reporter,.

Hearing is held. ARGUED. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

PLAINTIFFS SHACUAN SETO, GORDON LEE'S Motion To Tax Csaas Memorandum Of Costs Declaration Of Fredrick A Hagen

Plaintiffs' motion to tax the CSAA insurers' costs in this insurance bad faith and legal malpractice case is denied.

CSAA made CCP 998 offers of costs waiver - offers plaintiffs did not accept. Plaintiffs then lost their case to summary judgment, and CSAA filed a costs memorandum. Plaintiffs challenge one element of that memo: $14,790 for two experts - in insurance bad faith and attorney standard of care. Plaintiffs' arguments are unavailing.

First, plaintiffs say the CCP 998 offers were "a token or bad faith offer," but fail to carry their burden with analysis or authority. (Memo. 6:4-9; Adams v. Ford Motor Co. (2011) 199 Cal.App.4th 1475, 1484 [offeree's burden to prove bad faith].) Where, as here, a case's result is worse for offeree than the offer, this is "prima facie evidence showing the offer was reasonable." (Calvo Fisher & Jacob LLP v. Lujan (2015) 234 Cal.App.4th 608, 629.) Moreover, even modest offers may be in good faith if it is believed defendant has a significant likelihood of prevailing. (Jones v. Dumrichob (1998) 64 Cal.App.4th 1258, 1264.)

Second, plaintiff urges taxing because CSAA's experts did not testify. (Memo. 6:10-28.) However, "fees may be awarded for experts who [as here] aid in preparation for trial even if they do not actually testify." (Edmon & Karnow, California Practice Guide: Civil Procedure Before Trial (TRG 2016) 12:699.)

Finally, the experts fees were reasonable - indeed, lower than would be expected.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling in compliance with CRC3.1312.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer, Clerk: W. Trupek, Court Reporter: Nancy Presant, CSR 906, 510-751-8550, npresantcsr@gmail.com, Reported. (302/RBU)

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Of Seto, Lee And Lum Claims Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication; **Court Reporter Fees Paid**
Off Calendar May-27-2022 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
DEFENDANTS DAVID SAMUELSEN and BENNET, SAMUELSEN, REYNOLDS, ALLARD, COWPERTHWAITE'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Of Seto, Lee And Lum Claims Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication.

APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Frederick A. Hagen, Esq. (925.963.6796; fhagen@berdingweil.com) of Berding & Weil LLP for Plaintiffs, Shacuan Sharon Seto, et al.
Gregor A. Hensrude, Esq. (916.444.7573; GHensrude@klinedinstlaw.com) of Klinedinst PC for Defendants David Samuelsen, et al.
Ward Loveland, Esq. (650.592.5400; rloveland@chdlawyers.com) of Coddington, Hicks, & Danforth for Defendants, CSAA, et al.

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Defendant David Samuelsen and Bennet, Samulesen, Reynolds, Allard, Cowperthwaite & Gelini (collectively "Defendants")'s motion for summary judgment is granted.

As a preliminary matter, the Court takes judicial notice of the Schrieber v. Lee Court of Appeal of California, First Appellate District, Division One, Opinion filed on April 9, 2020 and the Operating Agreement of Golden Prosperities, LLC dated October 1, 2005, as attached to Sharon Sacuan Seto's declaration filed on August 7, 2014 in San Francisco Superior Court case No. CGC-14-539035 (Code Civ. Proc., Sec. 452; Exs. A and B.)

This case arises from the underlying premises liability action Martha Schreiber v. Stephen K. Lee Enterprises, et al., San Francisco Superior Court case no. CGC 13-534301 (the "Schreiber" action). There, the Court of Appeal held, "In short, in contrast to the vicarious liability the Lee children bore for any acts of their property manager under the nondelegable duty doctrine, they were not liable, regardless of their own fault (i.e., their own duty of care and breach thereof), for Lee's development and construction work." (Schreiber v. Lee (2020) 47 Cal.App.5th 745, 758; UMF 1.)

In this case, to prevail on a professional negligence claim against an attorney, the former client must prove (1) the breach of the applicable duty of care and (2) proximate causation between the conduct and the resulting injury. (See Paul v. Patton (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1088, 1095; Younan v. Caruso (1996) 51 Cal.App.4th 401, 408-409.) The proximate causation element must be proved under the "but for' test, meaning that the harm or loss would not have occurred without the attorney's malpractice." (Viner v. Sweet (2003) 30 Cal.4th 1232, 1235, 1241.) However, the Court of Appeal found, Plaintiff's "were not liable, regardless of their own fault (i.e., their own duty of care and breach thereof), for Lee's development and construction work." (Lee, supra, at 758.) Moreover, none of the Lee siblings have paid any money to their father or his estate. (SSUMF 2; Ex. E: Dep. of S. Seto at 121:12-15; Ex. F: Dep. of G. Lee at 62:1-22.) The only indemnity provision Planitff's cite to cannot create an indemnity obligation for Plaintiff's, as it t requires indemnity for Lee's actions as a member of the LLC, for LLC related conduct. (Ex. B: GPM Op. Agmt., 10.1.) Regardless, all of the claims presented by Plaintiffs fail given that they are released per a binding settlement agreement signed in October 2015.

Prevailing party to submit a proposed order that is verbatim with the substantive portion of the Court's ruling and in compliance with CRC 3.1312.
